content.full_text:
ST A TE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Jamie Raymond 
Chief Deputy City Attorney 
400 S. Vicentia Avenue, Suite 310 
Corona, CA 92882 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-15-216 
Dear Mr. Raymond: 
February 4 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Corona Mayor Eugene 
Montanez, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
May Mayor Montanez take part in decisions regarding the City's support or opposition of 
Southern California Edison's ("SCE") application before the PUC to construct a substation and high 
voltage electric lines in the City? 
CONCLUSION 
Based upon the facts provided, the financial effect of the SCE project would not have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's financial interests. Therefore, he 
does not have a conflict of interest in decisions involving the City's review of the SCE project. 
FACTS 
SCE is proposing to construct a substation and high voltage electric lines within the City in 
order to address growth and increased electrical usage, and improve reliability for SCE's customers 
in the cities of Corona, Eastvale and Norco. The proposed substation site is approximately ½ to ¾ 
of a mile from Mayor Montanez's printing business and real property located at 127 Radio Road. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-15-216 
Page No. 2 
High voltage lines will also be located on the south side of East Sixth Street, which is within 500 
feet of Mayor Montanez's building and printing business. 
There are no existing high voltage electric lines in this area of the City. These new high 
voltage lines are designed to provide power to the new substation. This type of project would 
typically not require the closure of the street on which the lines would be located (Sixth Street) or 
the street on which Mayor Montanez's business is located (Radio Road). However, it could require 
a lane closure on Sixth Street, usually during hours that avoid peak traffic periods. No work is 
planned on Radio Road. 
The zoning in the area is principally manufacturing. However, it is possible that the power 
lines would be visible from Mayor Montanez's business. 
SCE's project does not require approval or a pennit from the City, but does require the 
approval of the California Public Utilities Commission ("PUC"). The City is considering whether to 
support or challenge SCE's application before the PUC. The City's main consideration is that of 
timing and the possible need for more public outreach before an application is submitted. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
Section 87103 defines interests from which a conflict of interest may arise and includes among 
those interests: 
• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
interest worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b ). ) 
• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
investment worth at least $2,000. (Section 87103(a).) 
• Any source of income amounting to a total of at least $500, provided or 
promised to, and received by the public official within 12 months before 
the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• An official's personal finances, including those of his or her immediate 
family, also known as the .. personal financial effects" rule. (Section 
87103.)2 
2 Effects on an official's personal finances are not considered separately from the effect on an official's interest 
in a business entity or real property. (Regulation l 8702.5(c).) Thus, we do not consider the personal financial effects 
rule further. 


File No. A-15-216 
Page No. 3 
Based on the information you provided, Mayor Montanez has the following interests: 
• An interest in real property resulting from his ownership of a building on Radio Road. 
(Section 87103(b).) 
• An interest in his printing business as a business entity, resulting from his direct or indirect 
investment of $2,000 or more into the business, (Section 87103(a)) and an interest in the 
business as a source of income (Section 87103(c)). 
• Interests in any customers or clients of the printing business as sources of income. Income 
of an individual includes a pro rata share of the income of a business entity or trust in which 
the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a IO percent interest or 
greater. (Section 82030(a).) Therefore, customers or clients of the mayor's business who are 
sources of income to his business are also considered sources of income to him to the extent 
that his share of the income will be $500 or more. 
You have not identified sources of income to the mayor's business. However, if he receives 
income from sources of $500 or more, they may also be the basis of a conflict of interest. To the 
extent that the decision implicates a source of income to his printing business, the mayor should 
seek additional advice providing the relevant facts. For purposes of this analysis, we focus on the 
mayor's business and real property interests. 
Foreseeability: A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard 
for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. An 
interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the 
governmental decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) Under the facts you present, neither the mayor's real 
property interests nor his business entity interests are explicitly involved in the decisions before the 
City regarding the SCE application to construct a substation and high voltage electric lines within 
the City. 
In these cases, the foreseeability standard that applies is: "A financial effect need not be 
likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized 
as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." 
Materiality: 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. 
The Mayor's Printing Business: 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides that an effect on a business entity (including a business 
entity that is a source of income) is material as follows: 


File No. A-15-216 
Page No. 4 
"[The] financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient 
information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial 
effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly 
traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity." 
Examples of decisions that may affect the value of a privately held business entity include 
decisions to: 
• 
Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, 
traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or 
permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives. 
• 
Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other project that 
would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the 
project. 
The SCE project would not require the closure of Sixth Street nor Radio Road, where the 
Mayor's business and real property are located. It would require a lane closure on Sixth Street. 
Although the power lines would be visible from the mayor's business, the high voltage lines would 
not significantly change the character of the neighborhood, as the area is principally zoned for 
manufacturing. 
Based on the facts you have provided, the financial effect (whether negative or positive) of 
the SCE project appears nominal, if any. Therefore, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
would contribute to a change in the value of the mayor's privately held business entity.3 Therefore, 
his business and investment interests do not give rise to a conflict of interest in the SCE application 
decisions. 
The Mayor's Interest in Real Property {127 Radio Road): 
Regulation l 8702.2(a} provides a list of circumstances under which the reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on real property in which an official has a 
financial interest is material. As applicable to your facts, the financial effect will be material if the 
decisions: 
• 
Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or 
similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an interest will receive new or 
improved services that are distinguishable from improvements and services that are 
provided to or received by other similarly situated properties in the official's jurisdiction or 
where the official will otherwise receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the 
decision. (Regulation l 8702.2(a)(6).) 
3 The conclusions reached within the letter are based upon the facts provided. To the extent that there are any 
other facts not provided that suggest a potential effect on the business or value of the property, you should seek 
additional advice providing the relevant facts. 


File No. A-15-216 
Page No. 5 
• Would change the development potential of the parcel of real property. (Regulation 
I 8702.2(a)(7).) 
• Would change the income producing potential of the parcel of real property. (Regulation 
l 8702.2(a)(8).) 
• Change the highest and best use of the real property in which the official has an interest. 
(Regulation I 8702.2(a)(9).) 
• Change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic levels 
intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real property 
parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors 
that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a 
financial interest. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 0).) 
• Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 feet of the 
property line of the official's real property, other than commercial property containing a 
business entity where the materiality standards are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. 3 
(Regulation 18702.2(a)( 11 ).) 
• Cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property. 
( 18702.2(a)( 12).) 
Under the SCE proposal, the Mayor's real property will receive an upgrade in electrical 
power facilities and services. However, these new or improved services will not be distinguishable 
from improvements and services provided to or received by other similarly situated properties in the 
area, nor will Mayor Montanez receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision. 
Therefore, the presumptions in subdivision (a)(6) do not apply. 
The Mayor's property is within approximately½ to¾ of a mile from the proposed 
substation and within 500 feet of the high voltage power lines. However, the property at issue is 
commercial property containing a business entity. Therefore, the foreseeability and materiality 
presumptions in subdivision (a)( 11) do not apply. 
Moreover, it does not appear the SCE project will affect the mayor's use and enjoyment of 
the property as it will not result in road closures, and will only involve insignificant construction 
and temporary lane restrictions. 
While your facts indicate that the power lines may be visible from Mayor Montanez's 
business, the visual effects of the project are not likely to significantly change or impact an area that 
is zoned manufacturing, or is industrial in nature. 
Under these facts, we find that the financial effect of such a project would not have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the market value of the mayor's real property. 


File No. A-15-216 
Page No. 6 
Therefore, he does not have a conflict of interest in decisions involving the City's review of the 
SCE project. 
ER:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel

voltage electric lines in the City?

QUESTION: May Mayor Montanez take part in decisions regarding the City's support or opposition of 
Southern California Edison's ("SCE") application before the PUC to construct a substation and high 
voltage electric lines in the City?

CONCLUSION: Based upon the facts provided, the financial effect of the SCE project would not have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's financial interests. Therefore, he 
does not have a conflict of interest in decisions involving the City's review of the SCE project.
